data.title: D.R. Horton: This Homebuilder Has The Potential To Build Value
data.description:
If you're going to be an investor as opposed to a speculator, you better be ready for the volatility that comes with owning shares for an extended window of time. Even though I don't own shares in it, one example that I could point to involves a massive home builder by the name of D.R. Horton (NYSE:DHI). Back in December of last year, I decided to reaffirm the "buy" rating that I had on the stock. This was based on how cheap shares were and was in spite of the fact that there were signs of weakness on the horizon. Since then, the stock has tumbled by 17.7%. This is far worse than the 2.2% drop experienced by the S&P 500.

As disappointing as this is, the firm is still meaningfully outperforming the market since I originally rated it a "buy" way back in June of 2021. From that time through today, the S&P 500 is up a lofty 42.9%. But that pales in comparison to the 72.9% surge that shares of D.R. Horton experienced. Even though I fully expect weakness to continue in the near term, shares are still attractively priced on an absolute basis. Relative to similar companies, the stock is certainly on the pricier side of the spectrum. But even with this factored in, I would argue that the business is cheap enough to justify a "buy" rating.

Still a good home for investor money

In case you're not terribly familiar with D.R. Horton, the company operates as a massive homebuilder here in the US. According to management, the enterprise operates in 126 markets spread across 36 states. This includes major states like Pennsylvania, Florida, Texas, Georgia, and California. Since its inception back in 1978, the company has delivered over 1.1 million homes.

And the business claims that it operates as the largest builder in 62 different markets. This includes major areas like the Dallas-Fort Worth market where the company boasts an 18% market share, Houston where it has a 14% share of the market, Phoenix where it has a 12% share, San Antonio at 16%, Orlando at 16%, and Atlanta at 15%. The major market that it has the largest stake in, however, is Tampa. It has an astounding 24% market share there, though that still makes it the second-largest player in that area.

When scaling to this size, it's imperative to focus on as many customers as possible. Unlike some homebuilders, which focus more on higher end customers, D.R. Horton caters to more of a mass market buyer. 69% of the homes that it closed in the most recent quarter were priced at under $400,000. And only 12% of the homes closed were worth more than $500,000.

The most recent data that we have from management covers the second quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue came in at $7.73 billion. Even though this is an astounding amount of money, it's actually down 15.1% compared to the $9.11 billion reported one year earlier. This drop in revenue was driven by two factors. The biggest one was a decline in the number of homes closed from 22,548 to 19,276. At the same time, the company did experience a decline in the average price of a home from $375,500 to $372,500. The fact of the matter is that there has been weakness in the housing market. Nationwide, the median sales price of a new home was $403,600 in March of this year. That's down from the $436,400 reported the previous year. High interest rates and a cost-of-living crisis that exists in this country have negatively affected the industry.

The drop in revenue had a deleterious effect unprofitability as well. Net income plummeted from $1.17 billion to $810.4 million. All other important profitability metrics for the company worsened as well. Operating cash flow, for instance, went from negative $316.7 million to negative $436.2 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a decline from $1.25 billion to $958.9 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the company contracted from $1.51 billion to $1.08 billion.

In the chart above, you can see financial performance for the first half of 2025 compared to the same time of 2024. The drop in revenue, profits, and most cash flow metrics was driven by a decline in the number of homes closed from 41,888 to 38,335. Over the same window of time, the average price of a home closed by the company dropped from $375,800 to $373,700.

Even though I'm bullish about the company, there are some other indicators that are troubling. Take backlog as an example. At the end of the most recent quarter, the firm had 14,164 homes in backlog. That's a decline of 20.8% compared to the 17,873 homes that the business reported the same time one year earlier. To make matters worse, the total value of backlog has dropped because of not only the decline in the number of homes, but also because of a drop in the average price of a home in backlog from $393,900 to $386,700.

In all likelihood, the backlog will continue to shrink in the near term. I say this because net new orders of homes continue to come in weak. In the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, the company reported net new orders of 22,437 homes. That's a drop from the 26,456 homes reported the previous year. This rather substantial year-over-year decline brought the total number of homes ordered in the first half of this year down to 40,274 compared to the 44,525 reported for the first half of 2024. On top of this, the average price of a home ordered declined from $380,400 to $372,500. Cancellation rates are also ticking up slightly. For the first half of this year and last year, the cancellation rate for the business was 17%. But from the second quarter of 2024 to the second quarter of 2025, it increased slightly from 15% to 16%.

When it comes to the 2025 fiscal year in its entirety, management expects weakness to persist. They anticipate total deliveries of between 85,000 and 87,000 homes. At the midpoint, that would be down 4.1% from the 89,690 homes that the company closed in 2024. Management also expects revenue for the year to be between $33.3 billion and $34.8 billion. Even at the high end, this would represent a drop from the $36.80 billion the company reported last year. Not much was provided in the way of detail for profitability. The only thing that we know is that management is anticipating more than $3 billion in operating cash flow. If we annualize the results that we have seen so far for the year, we would get net profits of $3.71 billion, adjusted operating cash flow of $4.32 billion, and EBITDA of $4.93 billion.

Using these estimates, and also using the historical results for 2024, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. Even on a forward basis, shares of the business are trading at levels that I would consider to be reasonably attractive. But relative to similar companies, the stock is undoubtedly on the pricier end of the spectrum. In the table below, I stacked it up against five such firms. On a price to earnings basis, D.R. Horton ended up being the most expensive of the group. Four of the five companies ended up being cheaper than it is when using the EV to EBITDA approach. In fact, only on a price to operating cash flow basis is the business cheap relative to comparable enterprises. It ended up being the cheapest of the group using this approach.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
D.R. Horton 10.3 8.9 8.7
Taylor Morrison Home Corporation (TMHC) 6.8 14.7 6.0
Beazer Homes USA (BZH) 7.1 13.9 7.2
Toll Brothers (TOL) 8.1 11.6 7.2
Lennar (LEN) 8.0 16.9 6.5
PulteGroup (PHM) 7.2 13.4 5.4

Takeaway

To be honest with you, I think that difficult economic times are here again. I wouldn't be surprised if this makes things difficult on the homebuilding market for the next year or two. If tariffs are ultimately rescinded, I think the worst of the downturn could be avoided. But that doesn't seem terribly likely at the moment. For those who can't handle the volatility, it might be better to look elsewhere for opportunities. However, those focused on the long haul should view D.R. Horton in a favorable light. Management is optimistic enough that, in the first quarter of 2025, the company repurchased $1.3 billion worth of shares and paid out $125.5 million in the form of dividends. For the year in its entirety, the business is expected to buy back $4 billion worth of shares thanks to its new $5 billion share buyback program. This is all on top of the $500 million of dividends that management is forecasting. Given how cheap the stock is on an absolute basis, I would say this is a positive thing and it reinforces the ‘buy’ rating that I have on the business.

data.title: Cintas: Good Growth Prospects At Fair Value
data.description:
Cintas Corporation (NASDAQ:CTAS) provides uniform rental services, facility services, first aid and safety supplies, and fire protection solutions to over 1 million North American businesses. Cintas has achieved consistently positive revenue growth with a 10-year average of 8.77% and stable net margins with a 10-year average of 13.46%, except for 2010 and 2014, when it divested one of its business units called Shred-it.

We examine the company’s business model, by analyzing what drives Cintas’ revenue, exploring how cross-selling has benefitted the company by analyzing its segment performance and looking into specific examples of products and services between the segments. We also analyze the strong profitability, how Cintas has sustained it and how it has helped to support the company’s growth through fulfilling its capital expenditure requirements.

Market Drivers of Cintas’ Revenue

We identify what markets the company is in by compiling the products and services stated based on its annual report and compiling the market sizes of these products and services, as well as the growth forecast of these markets to analyze the total market growth outlook for Cintas.

While uniform rental and facility services are its core business, Cintas has a diversified revenue base comprising essential ancillary services that support other corporate functions such as compliance and workplace safety. From the chart, Cintas has a strong dominance (40%) in the Uniform Rental market, which makes up 55.13% of Cintas' revenue. Meanwhile, it has a smaller market share in the Soft Services Facilities Management market (0.39%), First Aid market (4.68%) and Fire Protections Systems and Other market (1.51%). We believe that the low market penetration in these three markets suggests that they are still underexploited by Cintas.

We highlight some drivers of growth across the markets:

  • Business growth: The number of business formations in the U.S. has grown at a 10-year historical average of 7.5% and has been positive in all years except for 2022 amid the slowdown in U.S. economic activity as GDP contracted for 2 quarters consecutively, and 2024 amid persistently high U.S. interest rates. Growth is driven by tax reforms that have lowered the cost of doing business and ease of access to capital, which has allowed companies to launch and scale operations with the necessary funding. These create a more favorable environment for new business growth, directly expanding Cintas’ total addressable market. As businesses are the core of Cintas’ customer base, growth in business formations could fuel demand across all of Cintas' revenue segments.
  • Employment: Total U.S. employment has grown at a 10-year historical average of 1% and was positive in all years except in 2020 amid the pandemic, growth has been largely driven by rising demand in sectors where Cintas’ uniform services are essential, such as healthcare and social assistance and leisure and hospitality. The private education and health services industry took up two-thirds of U.S. job creation in 2024, while the leisure and hospitality industry followed in second place. As employment expands in these uniform-dependent industries, it could fuel growth in Cintas’ existing customer accounts, as more employees could lead to higher uniform rental volumes and higher usage of ancillary services.
  • Regulatory Enforcement: Increasing regulatory enforcement from OSHA, such as through higher penalties for non-compliance, also drives the first aid market for certain industries. Companies demand training services where Cintas serves as a provider of workplace safety supplies and CPR/AED training services, under its Soft Services Facilities Management, First Aid, Fire Protection and Other segments.

Based on the chart, we believe that Cintas’ revenue is driven by a combination of both business formation growth and U.S. total employment growth. In 2020, despite a decline in U.S. total employment growth of 6.2%, this may have been mitigated by a 24.5% increase in business formation. Likewise, in 2024, the 4.7% decline in business formation may have been mitigated by a 0.7% increase in total employment. This contributed to Cintas sustaining positive revenue growth over the past 10 years.

We believe that the weighted average market projected CAGR of 6.1% bodes well for Cintas. This is as Cintas dominates the Uniform Rental market with an approximate 40% market share, outpacing uniform rental competitors like UniFirst (UNF) (17.6%), Elis (13.6%) and Vestis (VSTS) (8.6%). We believe this indicates an advantage for Cintas in the uniform rental market, due to its large scale and customer base of over 1 mln businesses.

Overall, we believe that Cintas is strongly positioned to capitalize on our calculated average market growth rate of 6.1%. This growth rate is also in line with Cintas’ historical 10-year average growth of 8.77%. We expect the uniform rental, software service facilities' management, first aid and fire protection systems markets growth to have a stable growth outlook, which in turn could allow Cintas to continue growing at a stable rate. We believe market growth is supported by growth in drivers such as rising U.S. business formation, employment growth and the necessity of safety compliance services. We expect continued growth in number of businesses growth in the U.S., as the U.S. ranks number 6 in terms of the Ease of Doing Business ranking by the World Bank. Potentially favorable tax policy which includes provisions like full expensing for machinery, R&D, generous interest deductions and lowered international tax rates could spur business growth, which would benefit Cintas as it could lead to a larger customer base growth opportunity. Employment is projected by the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics to grow at 0.4% CAGR through 2033, which could drive stable expansion in the customer base, particularly for its uniform rental business.

Cross-Selling Benefits

We examine whether the company has cross-selling opportunities across its diversified products/services segments and how it benefits its overall revenue. Based on its previous earnings transcript, management has stated that:

A majority of our customers take multiple services from us, and we continue to expand our penetration of cross-sold services.

All three business segments have shown consistently positive revenue growth except for 2015 and 2021 for the All Other segment. It has done this while maintaining its proportion of the revenue, indicating effective execution across the board. Overall, total revenue nearly doubled, growing from $4.37 bln in 2015 to $9.60 bln in 2024 at an average rate of 8.8%. We believe that the key driver behind this sustained expansion across all revenue lines is due to Cintas’ significant cross-selling opportunities.

Cintas targets overlapping customer groups across its revenue segments, creating strong cross-selling opportunities. From the table, each industry can utilize multiple Cintas offerings, such as how the gaming industry can employ work shirts, mats, safety and PPE, and fire sprinkler systems from Cintas, effectively contributing to all of Cintas’ revenue segments. As CEO Todd Schneider noted, Cintas identifies opportunities to “sell a vast majority of our products into even our rental customers”, suggesting that Cintas operates with its rental customers as a core customer base and focuses on driving growth by upselling and cross-selling additional products and services to this group. Industries that need uniforms also require facility services like cleaning supplies, restroom hygiene, and first aid and safety products. Similarly, these companies also need to comply with workplace safety regulations, necessitating the uptake of First Aid and Safety or Fire Protection Systems. By addressing the shared operational needs of customer segments, Cintas efficiently bundles complementary products, cross-selling multiple service lines to the same customer accounts.

Cintas boosts its customer penetration by making cross-selling a core part of its sales strategy, as noted by a sales representative at Cintas. Upselling and cross-selling additional products and services is also a key responsibility for their sales representatives, who are expected to assess customer needs and offer a broader suite of solutions beyond the initial service, such as introducing facility cleaning supplies or first aid products to a uniform rental client, while tailoring customization to suit the specific workflows of the industry.

Despite solid progress, management highlights in the Q2 2024 earnings call that they are “still in the very early innings of cross-selling across our business” and that they are focused on improving this area, suggesting significant upside remains in their cross-selling strategies. Cintas has stated that even among their current rental customers, many have not yet been offered or adopted other Cintas products like first aid kits; hence, we expect there are opportunities to sell more to existing rental clients. We believe this is evident as the company has a 40% market share in the Uniform Rental market, but it has a relatively small market presence in the Soft Facilities Management market (0.39%), First Aid market (4.68%) and Fire Protection and Other (1.51%) market, indicating that they have not yet fully penetrated these markets and that there is a large market opportunity which could represent continued cross-selling opportunities. We, therefore, believe that Cintas could cross-sell by either tailoring offers to customer needs or leveraging its mandatory First Aid and Safety services, like fire extinguishers and emergency lights.

We believe that Cintas can sustain its cross-selling strategy as it offers additional products and services to its existing customers, within and across its business segments. We believe this strategy has driven faster-than-market growth, particularly in adjacent segments like First Aid and Safety, as this segment has achieved a historical 10-year average growth of 14.1%, well above the market projected CAGR of 4.9%. We projected Copart’s sales growth in the table above, projecting growth in each segment based on its 10-year average of 8.7%, 14.1% and 6.6% respectively, resulting in projected 5-year forward average growth of 9% which is fairly in line with management’s guidance of 7.4% to 7.7% in 2025. We believe it is appropriate to continue growing at this rate, as we expect the company to benefit from market growth and be supported further by its cross-selling opportunities.

Strong Profitability Supporting Cintas’ Growth

In the next part, we examine the strong profitability of Cintas by analyzing its stable and increasing margins and how it has enabled it to fund its growth.

Cintas has demonstrated strong and stable margins over 10 years. Gross margin has remained in a consistent upward trend with an average of 45.7%, indicating efficient cost control. Net margin and EBIT margin have both shown an upward trend, averaging 13.5% and 17.8% respectively. This reflects enhanced operational and bottom-line performance. The free cash flow margin has also steadily improved, averaging 13.5%, suggesting a growing cash generation capacity.

Cintas’ strong positive profitability is primarily driven by its large-scale operations. Its large national footprint across North America enables economies of scale by spreading fixed costs over a large revenue base, driving a decline in unit costs. This is supported by its abundant quantity of sourced supplies. We believe this cost efficiency results in margin expansions as the company increases revenue without a proportionate rise in expenses. Cintas also benefits from its high route density where it operates approximately 11,700 local delivery routes, 467 operational facilities, and 12 distribution centers, well above its competitor Aramark with about 3,450 routes. High route density enables efficient delivery of products and services, lowering per-unit transportation and service costs across its route-based businesses, which make up the majority of its core revenue. These structural advantages reinforce its profitability and translate into sustained high margins.

Cintas invests in upgrading and expanding properties for its rental business and other product and service segments:

  • Rental Processing Plants: Clean and service uniforms, mats, and towels; include admin, sales, and service functions.
  • Rental Branches: Provide local admin, sales, and service operations.
  • First Aid and Safety Facilities: Stock, distribute, and manage first aid and safety products and services.
  • All Other Facilities: Include fire protection offices, direct sales offices, and other multi-segment operations.
  • Distribution Centers: Centralized inventory storage and distribution, including principal executive office in Cincinnati.
  • Manufacturing Facilities: Manufacture uniforms and related products.

As demand grows, additional facilities are necessary to increase their capacity to clean and service a larger volume of products while maintaining quality and customer responsiveness. This thereby ensures they can meet rising demand without operational bottlenecks. Cintas has stated plans to expand its operational facilities based on market demand rather than on a fixed schedule, focusing on growing where there is sufficient demand.

We believe that Cintas’ strong profitability has enabled it to sustain its capex levels, which in turn supports its revenue growth. Cintas primarily allocates capex to expand operational capacity through upgrading existing facilities, building new facilities and expanding into new markets, which supports growth as it allows them to gain capacity to service a greater customer segment. On average, Cintas generates $29.73 for every $1 of capex spent in the previous year with only a major outlier in 2022 with a higher revenue/capex of 54.74x, where the company reduced capex by 38% as management cited reduced capacity needs as revenue had yet to recover from COVID-19 disruptions. This highlights the strong use of capital investments translating into future revenue generation. Strong Revenue/Capex return is further supported by a strong Revenue/PPE ratio of 5.3x that has improved steadily across the period, suggesting productive use of fixed assets over time. Capex as a percentage of revenue is also observed to be consistent at a 4% average, which supports how we believe that strategic capex directly supports revenue growth by enabling capacity expansion and market penetration. Cintas funds these investments with an internally generated free cash flow margin averaging 13.5%, giving it the financial flexibility to reinvest in capacity expansion.

We believe that Cintas demonstrates strong profit margins that have been improving as the company grows larger in scale with its consistently positive revenue growth. We expect the company’s profitability to remain positive as we see the company continuing to grow with a 5-year forward revenue growth of 9% and its margins continuing to benefit from improved scale, leading us to model its net margins to increase from 17.6% in 2025 to 21.2% in 2029. The company management also highlighted investment in technology-driven efficiencies like SmartTruck, which optimizes routes thereby increasing operational efficiencies and partnerships with Google Cloud’s (GOOG) Vertex AI to decrease customer waiting times, which could support margin growth. In terms of FCF, we expect it to grow at a forward average rate of 13.3%, supported by stronger profit margins as well as stable capex spending, which has been low at only 4% of revenues despite the company’s ability to consistently grow revenues, highlighting its efficient use of capex to grow its business.

Risk: U.S. Tariffs Risk on Margins

Cintas faces potential margin erosion with the imposition or escalation of the U.S. tariffs on imported textiles and garments. While Cintas operates primarily within North America, Cintas and its suppliers source fabrics, raw materials, and finished goods from 3,000 international suppliers, including Honduras, India and Vietnam according to trademo, where imports from these countries are subject to a 10% baseline tariffs. If its suppliers raise prices to offset these tariffs, this translates to higher input costs for its uniform rental segment, which is its largest revenue segment. While Cintas can consider passing the costs on to consumers as businesses are not likely to be price-sensitive for this service, Cintas might choose to absorb the tariff impact to maintain long-term customer relationships. If tariffs remain high for too long, this could erode its margins.

Verdict

We believe that Cintas will continue to achieve positive consistent growth as it grows in line with the market, capturing incremental alpha through untapped cross-selling opportunities, and sustaining profitability through its large scale of operations, thereby allowing it to support the growth through necessary operational investments. We believe that these synergies will help Cintas sustain strong profitability that will support its revenue growth. Our revenue projections are based on the historical 10-yr averages for each of the revenue segments, resulting in a 5-year forward average growth rate of 9%. While slightly above the market CAGR of 6.1% and the management’s guidance of 7.4% to 7.7% in 2025, we expect the company to not just benefit from market growth but also explore further cross-selling opportunities. Our projections do not factor in any potential future acquisitions. The discount rate is 9.3% (OTC:WACC), and the terminal value is based on the company’s historical EV/EBITDA multiple of 25.51x, as there are limited comparable companies.

We derive a 5-year forward FCF margin of 19.88% which is a 1.69% increase from 2024 levels because we expect it to expand its scale and grow in margins. Despite the strong projected FCF growth of 13.3%, it currently trades at a forward EV/EBITDA of 32.36x, which is 27% above its 5-year average of 25.51x. We believe this suggests that the market could be too optimistic about its growth prospects and is expecting even stronger growth acceleration. Based on our DCF, we derived a limited upside of 1%. Hence, we rate Cintas as a Hold.

data.title: Booking Holdings: The Ultimate AI Agent For Travel
data.description:
Last April 16th, I published my first article on Seeking Alpha about Booking Holdings (NASDAQ:BKNG). It was probably a matter of timing, since it coincided with the market declines at the beginning of April, but the stock is up +21.79% compared to the index’s 11.31%.

That first article was a summary of the Booking thesis: becoming a one-stop shop for the travel and tourism industry by leveraging a critical mass of users and multiple verticals that feed into each other—for example: flights, hotels, alternative accommodations, airport transfer cars... Booking aims to be an all-in-one solution for any trip.

In my latest article, I also wrote the following:

"Booking has been a company I’ve truly enjoyed learning about, which is why I plan to continue covering it here on Seeking Alpha. Overall, it checks all the boxes of what I look for: a dominant market position, a favorable long-term industry trend, strong competitive advantages, solid management, and healthy fundamentals."

Over the past two months, I've continued researching and clarifying ideas that I’m going to present in this new article. It will be focused on how Booking is asserting itself over the competition, including several interesting comments made during their recent conference with Bank of America, which will help us better understand the company’s growth profile. And finally, what gives this article its title: how AI travel agents will completely transform the industry.

Booking, Ahead of Competition

I believe that, for now, companies like Booking, Airbnb, Inc. (NASDAQ:ABNB), and Expedia (NASDAQ:EXPE) will coexist. But in the tech-network-driven world we're heading toward—where a critical mass of users is key to a company's success—I think this is an industry that will tend toward a "winner takes all" dynamic.

This will happen due to a supply-and-demand network effect: the more hotels and alternative accommodations available, the more users will be attracted, and in turn, more hotels will want to list their properties. On top of this, you have to consider the rest of Booking’s verticals, which enhance the user's travel experience. Let's remember that Booking Holdings is made up of six subsidiaries.

  • Booking: Hotel and apartment bookings... now also including flights.

  • Price Line: A site offering discounts on hotels, flights, and rental cars, with a focus on last-minute deals.

  • Kayak: A travel search engine that compares prices for flights, hotels, and rental cars across various websites.

  • Agoda: A booking platform particularly strong in Asia, offering hotels, flights, and activities.

  • Rentalcars: A service specializing in rental car comparison and booking worldwide.

  • OpenTable: A platform to book restaurant tables, browse menus, and read customer reviews.

I find it quite brilliant how Booking managed to penetrate the European market more effectively than Expedia. Expedia used the Merchant model (which is the one Booking now uses, where the company receives the money upfront), while Booking originally used the Agency model (the customer doesn’t pay anything until arriving at the hotel, and the hotel then pays Booking its share). This Agency model was much more convenient for the user and greatly contributed to market penetration. Although it meant a few tougher years for Booking, the decision has more than paid off.

In 2024, Booking Holdings' Gross Booking Value reached $165 billion, while Expedia’s was $110 billion. In the same year, Booking Holdings reported an operating profit of $7.6 billion, compared to just $1.3 billion for Expedia. That’s a difference of more than 5 times in profits, despite Booking’s Gross Booking Value being only about 50% higher. Today, Booking Holdings' market capitalization is more than seven times greater than Expedia’s. When comparing margins, Expedia slightly outperforms Booking in gross margins (approximately 90% vs. 86%). However, when it comes to profitability metrics, the picture changes dramatically. Booking has a net margin of 25%, compared to just 9% for Expedia.

Airbnb is a different story. It’s a model much more focused on alternative accommodations. Its CEO is far more product-obsessed than execution-driven. It’s often acknowledged that Airbnb’s interface and presentation are much better than Booking’s—but I don’t think the gap is that wide. In contrast, Booking’s superior execution and capital allocation make a much bigger difference. Additionally, with a larger user base, the network effect is stronger. Booking is actively going after Airbnb in the alternative accommodations segment, holding a 35% market share compared to Airbnb’s 45%, even though Airbnb is almost entirely specialized in that category.

In terms of Gross Bookings for alternative accommodations: Airbnb generated $81 billion, while Booking reached $54 billion. Currently, 35% of bookings on Booking are for Alternative Accommodations (a category introduced in 2014). In fact, in the most recent quarter, these alternatives grew by 19% for Booking compared to 12% for Airbnb. Booking has outpaced Airbnb in growth in this segment in 15 of the last 16 quarters.

Regarding their strong capital allocation and cost management, as we discussed in the previous article, Booking’s management always reports figures like EBITDA excluding SBC (stock-based compensation), since they consider it a real expense. This disciplined capital and business management helped them avoid even a single quarter of negative sales—even during the peak of the crisis in March 2020. I think that says a lot about the quality of the company.

Part of this is likely due to their Genius loyalty program. I already explained the different tiers in the previous article, but in the conference—which we’ll discuss in the next section—they said the following:

So what we are seeing is in general, customers coming more direct to us, we see them coming more frequently to us. So more times per year, they do their bookings with us. We see them booking across more verticals. So in the past, maybe only for a hotel, now they do a hotel and alternative accommodation. And next time, they say, "Oh, I can also book a flight. Oh, it's actually was a good experience." And they come back again for that. So we see that.

Then we see them doing those connected trip or multi-vertical transactions for one trip. So moving up in the loyalty status level from Genius, getting more economic benefits coming out of that, and therefore, coming back more frequently and they do it more and more on the app. So all of that hangs together. It's all correlated to each other. So we're not really looking at loyalty as something that's standalone.

Booking Holdings Inc. Presents at Bank of America Securities 2025 Global Technology Conference

This week, Booking held a very interesting conference where the CFO, Mr. Ewout Lucien Steenbergen, shared valuable information that strongly reinforces the long-term view I had about the company.

At the beginning of the conference, they showcased how effective they are at integrating and pursuing new business verticals:

And we know that if Booking is going after opportunities and really puts a lot of effort and investments and management focus on it, it can really take off. I think we have proven that with flights. So we started to really focus on that the last 2, 3 years. And we are now one of the largest flight OTAs in the world. So that's, of course, incredible.

Regarding the ability to integrate new verticals and future possibilities, I was thinking about this some time ago: to truly integrate the entire travel experience into a single platform, you need to include experiences. For that, you could form partnerships with tourist attractions in cities—even with local governments. I believe this could create a lot of value for tourist destinations that want to attract visitors but aren’t yet well known (like Albania or North Macedonia).

If you integrate all leisure offerings a city has—both public and private—into the travel platform with the largest user base in the world (Booking), it’s a win-win. Your AI travel agent could book your flight, hotel, airport transfer, and even create a complete itinerary based on activities rated by other users and tailored to your past preferences. You could even partner with private apartments and alternative local accommodations in those cities to further strengthen the value proposition.

We put out a number about attractions. That is a new area that we focused on. We said 92% growth in the first quarter year-over-year. Still modest pace, but it's just showing how much more we can do.

As for the future growth we can expect from the company, it’s very much in line with the valuation I presented in my previous article.

Yes. So we said indeed 8%, 8%, 15%, so at least 8% revenue growth and growth -- bookings growth and then 15% EPS growth. And we think those percentages are achievable. If you look first at accommodations, in general, the accommodations market is growing in line with GDP plus-plus. So if you think about GDP, plus there is more growth usually in travel than GDP because if people -- when people get more income, they tend to spend more on travel, exploring the world. So that is additive. I know sometimes there is that perception of, well, because you are so large, therefore, your growth will be basically a proxy for the market growth. We are not believing in that. We think actually our growth opportunity is better than the market on average.

I’ve already mentioned earlier in the article why I like Booking’s management so much: they’re excellent at returning capital to shareholders, focused on cutting unnecessary costs while continuing to invest in the business, and, lastly, they are very transparent.

I think really putting more out there from a disclosure perspective to transparency. Let me give you an example. I mean we have been talking for a long time about Connected Trip. But last year, we said, "You know what, let's put some metrics around it because otherwise, people don't know, is it purely a concept and it isn't there in practice? Or is it really already there?" And we said, "We are in the high single digits in terms of Connected Trip," meaning someone that purchases for one trip more than one kind of vertical. So it could be a flight and a rental car or an accommodation and/or -- and an activity. So that's an important one. And we said high single digits and it's growing every period something like 30%, 40%. And it -- we will continue to give those disclosures.

The Ultimate AI Agent For Travel

We’ve reached the final section, dedicated to what gives the article its title.

I believe Booking will eventually disintermediate and replace any physical travel agent, competing directly with other platforms like Airbnb, which also have massive user bases. We’re moving toward a world of vertical search engines, and now that AI is easier to train, the key lies in generating proprietary data and owning the distribution. Both of these depend entirely on the size of your user base, creating a network effect: the more users you have, the more data is generated, leading to better-trained AIs (travel assistants), which in turn attract even more users—spinning the flywheel further.

AI agents will be a reality in the future, and I believe Booking will have the best one, thanks to the massive amount of proprietary data it possesses. The possibilities range from hotel searches with exact requirements, to itineraries, scheduled and interconnected bookings.

What’s most exciting is that if these ideas are occurring to me, when you listen to the CEO, who's been with the company for 18 years, you realize he’s already several steps ahead. He’s mentioned this in multiple interviews. For example, he often talks about how, if you’ve planned your entire trip through them and your flight is delayed, the agent should be able to automatically reschedule your airport transfer, move your dinner reservation, and offer real-time solutions—without wait times or needing to verify your personal information.

During the conference, they made many insightful comments that reinforce my belief that the management team is several steps ahead when it comes to envisioning possibilities that most investors can’t even imagine.

GenAI is, of course, opening up the world of a real connected trip in the sense of logic between what you're booking, what you have done in the past. So if you book a rental car, your hotel should have a parking place for you. And if your flight gets delayed, your restaurant reservation has to be pushed out and all the -- just the issues that are today, the messiness of travel and things that happen, the peace of mind that we can provide with GenAI. So that means even more, I think, benefit of being personalized peace of mind. And then people come more and more back, and that will then help also from a loyalty perspective. So that's, I think, what is opening up with GenAI.

We would like also to build our own travel-specific vertical agent because we want to make sure that customers are -- that are coming direct to us today will stay with us as direct customers. And they want to use our agent because this agent is the most knowledgeable about travel, has the most insight in your personal preferences. Your data is coming from a company that customer knows, trusts and know where to go to if something goes wrong. Yes, you can go to a generic agent. But if it goes wrong, where did it book ultimately your trip? And where should you go to if there is a problem? Or who is touching your money? So we can take care of all those trust factors because we have the relationship, the trust of our customers. So we're working on all of those, and then we'll see how things will play out from a scenario perspective over time.

Finally, all of this not only helps improve Booking’s overall product, but also enhances the user experience, customer service, and leads to greater cost savings and overall operating leverage. They’re investing in the present to become significantly more profitable in the future.

So for example, what we're doing with generative AI in customer service. Think about our agents -- well, first of all, do you still need to speak to a human? I think over time, I think some of those large language models is incredibly human-like, very natural and that will -- for simple matters. You don't need to wait in line anymore. You get a better customer experience, faster and more efficient. For more complex matters, yes, you still mainly need a human agent but can find the information much quicker, doesn't need to spend the time writing notes afterwards. That's a big benefit. That's what we are going after.

Conclusion

I’m very pleased with everything I continue to learn about Booking. My research keeps reinforcing the fact that we’re looking at a truly outstanding company. The valuation remains unchanged—it hasn’t shifted in the past two months. I really appreciate seeing how the management is so focused on improving the product, cutting costs, investing in the future, and properly rewarding shareholders. I’ll continue learning and covering Booking here.

data.title: HIO: Limited Appeal Due To Poor NAV Growth And Weak Dividend Coverage
data.description:
Overview

Western Asset High Income Opportunity Fund (NYSE:HIO) operates as a closed end fund that aims to provide attractive total returns from a portfolio of high-yield corporate debt securities. I've grown fond of this type of debt investing because it offers a hedge against the volatility that can be experienced from traditional equities. However, I would only recommend these sorts of income-oriented investments to investors that specifically want to put a focus on income generation. This is because funds like HIO typically underperform traditional equities over time, which can be related to the emphasis on income generation. If you are an investor that seeks to maximize the total return within your portfolio, I would look elsewhere.

Looking at the performance over the last 12 months, we can see that HIO's price has increased by about 2.1%. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps to 14.3% over the same time frame. The main appeal of holding a position in HIO is the generous starting dividend yield of approximately 11%. This double-digit yield and the monthly payouts make it an enticing choice for the investors that are looking to create a sizable stream of supplemental income. However, recent reporting leaves me a bit cautious about the sustainability of the distributions at this moment.

HIO has an inception dating back to 1993, which means that we have over three decades worth of performance data to reference. Additionally, the fund is actively managed by Franklin Templeton Fund Adviser and has a management fee of 0.80%. Since HIO focuses on corporate debt securities, the fund has demonstrated a clear weakness to the higher interest rate environment. I think that interest rates will remain elevated for the remainder of 2025 and this limits the appeal of initiating a position at the current valuation. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying fund strategy and investment methodology that HIO implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the most recent fact sheet, HIO has total assets of $384M that is spread across a diverse range of debt securities. The fund utilizes extensive credit research methods to identify debt securities that may be attractively priced. As a result, we can see that Consumer Cyclical investments make up the largest bulk of the fund at 21.44%. This is followed by exposure to Communications and Energy investments, accounting for 17.46% and 14.57% of assets respectively. These top three sector allocations make up a combined total of more than 53.4% of assets, so we can assume that these will have the greatest impact on HIO's daily price movement and total return potential.

The positions within HIO have an average life of 6.7 years, which indicates that income potential can remain fairly stable for the foreseeable future. However, this is assuming that there are limited prepayments. HIO also has an average coupon of 7.2%. These sort of debt securities can offer some resilience during market downturns.

For instance, markets declined throughout March and April because of President Trump's tariff announcements. On a YTD basis, the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) has recovered and has a total return of 2%. However, we can see that HIO slightly outperforms and has a total return of 3.5%, while also experiencing less of a decline during the initial market drop.

However, I wouldn't anticipate HIO showing this sort of outperformance over the long term. In fact, the emphasis on income generation will usually lead to underperformance. This is because HIO will continue to prioritize paying out its distributions, even during market periods where the underlying strategy didn't actually generate enough income to support it, which leads to NAV deterioration. As expected, we can see how HIO underperforms traditional markets over the longer time horizon of 10 years.

Valuation

Since HIO operates as a closed end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of the underlying net assets. As a result, HIO currently trades at a small discount to NAV of about 3.5%. However, this discount level isn't that attractive when we consider the fact that HIO has traded at an average discount to NAV of 8% over the last three-year period. Looking back over the last 10 years, we can see that HIO has frequently traded within the same discount to NAV range, which could be an indication of some underlying flaw related to the fund's strategy.

In an effort to get better clarity around the current valuation and shrinking discount to NAV level, I reviewed the most recent semi-annual report dated as of December 2025. Net investment income amounted to $0.16 per share, while the fund experienced net realized losses of $0.14 per share. Looking back over prior years, we can see that net investment income levels have remained within a pretty consistent range. Since this is the semi-annual report, we can assume that HIO will double the current earnings rate, which would leave us with an estimated net investment income of $0.32 per share by the end of the full year of 2025.

However, I am a bit unsatisfied with the level of net realized gains the fund has experienced. Over the last five years, HIO has experienced net realized losses more frequently than it has seen gains. Therefore, I worry about the fund's ability to participate in positive market momentum. I believe that the inconsistent performance here has contributed to HIO's NAV decline in recent years. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts going back to 2020:

  • 2020: $5.27 per share.
  • 2021: $5.45 per share.
  • 2022: $4.22 per share.
  • 2023: $4.15 per share.
  • 2024: $4.23 per share.
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $4.04 per share.

Since HIO's net realized gains are already negative for this first portion of 2025, I assume that performance won't substantially improve by the end of the fiscal year. Therefore, I anticipate that HIO's NAV will continue its downward slide in the short term. The declining NAV is one of the clearest indications that a fund's strategy isn't efficiently generating the earnings it needs, which makes it very difficult to recommend a position in HIO.

However, the overall appeal of the fund can be a bit tricky. HIO's price has declined by more than 68.4% since its inception. However, the total return including all distributions currently sits above 454%! Whether or not this type of capital deterioration is worth the tradeoff is up to investors to decide, but I do not think the value proposition is attractive in my opinion.

Risk Profile

The primary source of risk for a fund like HIO comes from the large exposure to debt securities that have poor credit ratings. For reference, credit ratings that are below a BBB are classified as below investment grade, otherwise known as 'junk bonds'. Borrowers with poor credit ratings typically have less consistent cash flows, rely more heavily on debt, and are less resilient to challenging market conditions. The usual relationship is that the lower the credit rating, the higher the chance of borrower default. HIO's breakdown is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 13.91%
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - C): 83.39%
  • Not Rated: 2.10%

As you can see, HIO is primarily exposed to these below investment grade debt securities, which increases the overall risk for the fund. These risks are further increased by the higher interest rate environment. Looking at the graph below, we can see how the federal funds rate and HIO's price share an inverse relationship. When interest rates were cut to near zero levels following the pandemic, HIO's price rapidly increased. However, HIO's price remains heavily suppressed since interest rates were hiked to their decade highs throughout 2022 and 2023. A higher interest rate directly translates to a higher cost of debt.

A higher cost of debt negatively impacts operating margins for borrowers and makes it more difficult to grow. The higher interest expenses that borrowers need to pay, can also contribute to the rise in defaults as it becomes more difficult to keep up with the required debt maintenance. S&P Global estimates that the rate of defaults could increase to 1.6% in a base scenario by December 2025. However, I believe that the uncertainty around interest rate cuts and the tariff situation puts us near the pessimistic scenario, which estimates that defaults could rise as high as 3% by December 2025.

I believe that future interest rate cuts can serve as a strong positive catalyst for growth at some point in the future. Lower rates would increase optimism around debt securities and would result in the fear of defaults. Unfortunately, I don't believe interest rate cuts are on the horizon. I anticipate that the rise in costs due to tariffs will slow economic growth and cause an unfavorable shift in consumer spending. Inflationary data also remains slightly higher than the Fed's targeted 2% range, which is one of the reasons that the Fed decided to leave interest rates unchanged during their last meeting. Therefore, a higher for longer interest rate environment can limit the appeal of entry here and cause HIO's price to decline further if defaults increase.

Distribution Sustainability

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.0355 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 11%. If we multiply this monthly payout by 12, we get an estimated annual payout amount of $0.426 per share. Referring back to the same semi-annual report, it would appear that HIO has a very inconsistent history of generating a sufficient amount of capital to support the distributions. Just for reference, here are the annual earnings amounts over the last few years:

  • 2020: $0.14 per share
  • 2021: $0.54 per share
  • 2022: ($0.87) per share
  • 2023: $0.30 per share
  • 2024: $0.51 per share
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $0.02 per share

As we can see, HIO has only been able to out earn its distribution amount in two out of the last five years. The poor distribution coverage can certainly lead to a distribution cut in the near time if conditions do not improve. Looking over the payout history over the last decade, we can see that HIO has experienced a handful of reductions and raises in the past. Therefore, I wouldn't be surprised to see the fund reduce its distribution back to its prior 2023 levels to help preserve further NAV decay.

It's also worth mentioning that the distribution received from HIO is typically funded by net investment income. Therefore, the distributions would be mostly classified as ordinary dividend income, which has the heaviest tax burden for investors. So, based on your specific tax bracket, amount of capital invested, and desired income needs, a position in HIO would be best utilized within a tax advantaged account to offset any potential tax burdens.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I rate HIO as a sell for now. The underlying performance has been weak, but this could be due to the elevated interest rate environment. I do believe that lower interest rates can serve as a catalyst for growth at some point in the future, but the current environment does not warrant the need for cuts at this time. If we remain in a higher for longer interest rate environment, this could elevate the concerns around the underlying rate of defaults. Borrower defaults would negatively impact earnings and may cause HIO's price to fall further. The fund has demonstrated poor NAV growth over the last year and very inconsistent distribution coverage from earnings.

data.title: Fielmann: Margin Recovery Likely, But Still Not Priced In
data.description:
A Multi-Year Investment Cycle Causing Some Trouble

When a European mid-cap founder-lead business starts a multi-year investment cycle, investors should pay attention. The usual unfolding of events often creates very interesting investment opportunities – although, this must be said, a lot of patience is required until the time is right to invest.

I have recently presented the case of another family-founded and family-managed German mid-cap at the end of its investment cycle and waiting for benefits to show up in the numbers; today I will present the similarly interesting case of Fielmann (OTCPK:FLMNF) (OTCPK:FLMNY).

Fielmann is one of the leading vision care providers worldwide, which, based on its rock-solid 51% market share in Germany, is expanding into many other European countries and in the U.S.

Fielmann aims to be a vertically integrated, low-cost provider of optical care that uses its scale advantages and high-throughput shops to build a nearly unassailable competitive moat. To make ends meet, competitors are either forced to opt for a service-intensive, small-volume, luxury market niche or for a lower quality, mass product shop model, which fails sooner or later.

If you are not familiar with the business, you can find a recent company presentation here.

Six years ago, when the founder of the company passed the baton to his young son, a huge international expansion was announced and then, step by step, executed through a string of M&A transactions, coupled with massive investments into a multi-channel sales and logistics platform. At the same time, Germany precipitated into a multi-year stagnation, pressuring consumer confidence, while the Ukraine war and the European energy crisis caused sky-high inflation rates.

As a result, the once steadily growing, reliable dividend stock reversed course. The dividend was cut to free cash for further expansion, while margins shrank and volumes in the all-important German home market stagnated. The stock, of which the founding family still holds 73%, is not extremely liquid and used to be held by thousands of retail investors looking for stable dividends in the ZIRP years. Clearly, the stagnating and sometimes even shrinking earnings, coupled with a dividend cut and some unavoidable hiccups related to the expansion into foreign markets could not raise their confidence level. So the investor base changed at a time of maximum uncertainty.

This is why, until very recently, the stock hovered around the €40 level, down about 50% from its 2017 peak.

Then a series of solid earnings releases were issued, which were, however, clouded by uncertainties around the recent U.S. expansion. Only the most recent Q4 2024 release and Q1 2025 update brought back some confidence in the new, rejuvenated management's margin targets – sending the stock some 45% higher within a few weeks.

Management now expects to reach its margin targets in 2025, originally announced together with the multi-year expansion cycle years ago, and will present its 2030 financial targets in July at its AGM.

What Is Priced In

At the current price of €56, the company has a market cap of €4.7B and will be almost completely debt-free by year-end (consensus net debt/EBITDA expectation = 0.38). Consensus expects an EBT margin of 11.7%, growing roughly 1% per year for the next two years. EPS is expected to reach €2.34 in 2025 (P/E 24) and €3.03 in 2027. The important dividend will finally move higher again (Fielmann used to pay out almost its entire profits prior to the investment cycle) and should increase some 50% from last year's level until 2027. At that point, analysts still don't see Fielmann returning to its previous almost full payout policy, as the payout ratio would still be just over 56%.

Assuming further, albeit somewhat slower margin expansion until 2030, we might project Fielmann to reach sales of €3.2B (CAGR just over 5%, not much more than the expected optical market growth rate), an EBT margin of 15% and EPS of €4 in 2030. At a 25x multiple, the stock would trade for €100. Together with some €8 of likely dividends, we would almost double our money in five years (93% total return).

As this would be an excellent level of return for a generally low-risk, high-multiple sector, it seems that the market is still a bit skeptical regarding further growth and margin expansion. Investors probably first want to see the company's multi-year financial targets before paying up.

How Justified Is Further Caution?

Clearly there is some potential for negative surprises, if management announces further expansion projects, leading to another cycle of margin compression, or if financial targets are below consensus expectations. This would probably lead to a stagnating or falling stock price in the near term. Over the long term, some deeper analysis is required to gauge where the company is headed.

Looking at my multi-year Excel overview, one key factor stands out: personnel expenses in Germany.

In 2017, Germany delivered 83% of total company EBT, and its personnel expenses were 39.2% of sales. Fast-forward to 2024, personnel expenses in Germany have ballooned to 45.8% of sales, thus reducing Germany's EBT margins by 6.6%. Context: For about a decade prior to 2017, Fielmann had always delivered a total company EBT margin of about 17%, with its Germany segment at a similar level. Today, Fielmann's overall EBT margin is just 9.9% and Germany comes in at 12.2%, while delivering 79% of EBT.

In a nutshell: If Fielmann could reduce its personnel expense ratio back to where it stood in 2017, its overall EBT margin would recover as well.

This is obviously a very simplified analysis, as there are lots of other moving parts. E.g., the costly expansion into Spain and the U.S. has reduced EBT-margins, while scale efficiencies have reduced the material cost ratio, thus compensating some of the start-up costs.

Yet, personnel expenses in Germany remain a key factor to watch.

They have increased because of general high wage inflation in Germany, not because of a greater shop network or higher sales: Employee numbers and volumes have roughly stagnated over the past 7 years in Germany. In addition, there was a substantial lack of optometrists looking for new jobs coupled with many free positions, as can be seen in this statistical chart created by the German category association:

The blue line shows the number of jobless optometrists, the other line shows the available job openings. Luckily for Fielmann, after 2022 the situation improved: There are now 30% less job openings and 20% more unemployed optometrists. There is still no equilibrium, however, which is why there will probably remain at least some upward pressure on wages.

My scuttlebutt research tells me that Fielmann pays more than its competitors, which is the smart thing to do, as it still enjoys far higher profit margins and higher sales/employee. Hence, it can afford higher wages, while its competitors, if they pay more for their employees, will have to raise prices. And this reinforces Fielmann's key competitive advantage: being the low-cost provider. It will lead to continued market share gains.

Just consider that Apollo Optik, which is part of the international GrandVision shop network and Fielmann's key competitor in Germany, before it was taken over by Essilor Luxottica (OTCPK:ESLOF) (OTCPK:ESLOY) used to need about 10x as many shops to produce roughly the same net income of Fielmann.

This is why I believe that margin pressure from personnel costs is probably here to stay, but will likely be at least somewhat compensated by greater pricing power, share and scale gains, alongside other optimization efforts.

For example, Fielmann has been investing for years in a new logistics hub in the Czech Republic. It has expanded operations in the Rathenow site, enabling more of its German shops to avoid lengthy manual operations on newly ordered glasses, which will be delivered ready-to-use from the central manufacturing hub. All this is just starting now and will lead to greater productivity of the "expensive" shop optometrists it has just employed.

At the same time, key competitors like the online optical retailer Mr. Spex are struggling and more and more of the small, owner-run optical shops in Germany will be closed once the founder retires, leaving their market share up for grabs. According to the category association, 36% of all optical shops are run by owners older than 60 years. This is massive.

This situation is clearly one of the many good reasons to employ as many young optometrists as possible, as very soon there will be lots of market share up for grabs.

All in all, I believe Fielmann has an excellent chance to profit nicely from its multi-year investment cycle, even if profit margins won't recover to previous levels in the near term.

This is also unlikely because margins in the U.S. are structurally lower, the new markets still need lots of integration efforts and expenses, several small European markets are still unprofitable, and the multi-channel platform Fielmann has been building over the past seven years is still not completed.

But it is easy to see how much low-hanging fruit will be reaped sooner or later. And Fielmann has clearly stated its goal to be margin leader alongside price leader. Management's focus now is on margin expansion and reaping profits from the recent investments.

Conclusions

Businesses controlled by their founding families are known to have a long planning horizon, much longer than the usual stock market investor. As I said at the beginning of this article, such a divergence creates opportunities, when the family confronts the stock market with a multi-year profit slump, a dividend cut and margin compression – all coupled with the promise of becoming a better business a year down the road. Few investors are patient enough to stay the course and keep their confidence, as inevitable missteps hit the wire and guidances are missed. So the stock crashes – not because of fundamental problems, but rather because of a too ambitious (and sometime hard to understand) growth investment cycle.

At the same time, the company's strategy can be sound, it just requires some "capacity to suffer."

In the case of Fielmann, my company, market and financial analysis shows the excellent long-term reasons for the family's decision to sacrifice some margin in Germany, to internationalize the business in order to increase its scale and efficiency (which is already compensating for some of the margin compression in its home market and is likely to deliver even more benefits).

All in all, I believe Fielmann is very likely to deliver at least the level of growth projected by consensus, as the sell-side, after many years of unsatisfactory stock returns and lots of unusual volatility, is probably a bit too cautious.

This means that, over the next few years, investors should be able to more than double their money by investing in the stock, even after the recent run-up. This projection doesn't require much of a multiple expansion from the current 25x, although in the good times, Fielmann used to trade for well over 30x earnings. In the near term, the July 10th AGM will provide clarity about the company's 2030 financial targets – and I believe management will be ambitious, and the market should like it.

If you decide to buy and don't need tens of thousands of shares, the German exchanges offer excellent liquidity, while the U.S. over-the-counter marketplaces are usually rather inefficient. Therefore, it is preferable to use an international broker like Interactive Brokers (IBKR).

U.S. investors should also be aware of the substantial exposure to Euro-based earnings. In case of USD weakness, this should be a positive, but could obviously also reduce returns, if the Euro loses its recently gained relative strength.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best International Stock Idea investment competition, which runs through June 6. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Eton Pharmaceuticals: Rapid Revenue Growth And Pipeline Catalysts Point To More Upside
data.description:
Eton Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (NASDAQ:ETON) has rapidly transformed from a micro-cap obscurity into a fast-growing rare-disease pharmaceutical player, delivering over 400% share price appreciation in the past year. The company’s strategic pivot to commercializing specialty rare-disease treatments is translating into explosive top-line growth, with revenue more than doubling year-over-year. Despite the stock’s impressive run, ETON’s forward valuation still offers compelling risk/reward, supported by: (1) continued high double-digit revenue growth from new and existing rare disease assets; (2) improving gross margins and operating leverage as scale is achieved; and (3) a unique asset-light model that requires modest incremental investment. While the company is not yet sustainably profitable and carries notable leverage, near-term catalysts—including product launches, label expansions, and potential M&A—should drive upside going forward. Even after the recent re-rating, ETON trades at a discount to high-growth specialty pharma peers on sales multiples, presenting a tactical buy for growth-oriented investors willing to tolerate volatility and execution risk.

Company Overview

Eton Pharmaceuticals, Inc. is focused on commercializing and developing rare disease therapies, including Increlex, Alkindi Sprinkle, Galzin, PKU Golike, Carglumic Acid, Betaine Anhydrous, and Nitisinone. The company also maintains a late-stage pipeline featuring several potential value drivers such as ET-400 for adrenal insufficiency, ET-600 for diabetes insipidus, Amglidia for neonatal diabetes, and others.

Recent Results and Financial Position

For the trailing twelve months ending in Q1 2025, Eton reported revenue of $48.3 million, reflecting a year-over-year increase of 117%. Gross profit was $28.3 million, corresponding to a gross margin of 58.5%. While the operating margin improved significantly to -2.7%, it remains negative as research and development, along with selling, general, and administrative expenses, have increased with new product launches. The company posted a net loss of $4.6 million, or an EPS of -$0.18. EBITDA was positive but minimal at $3,000, while normalized EBITDA was -$342,000. Free cash flow was negative at -$2.6 million, and levered free cash flow remains negative due to recent investments and working capital growth. As of Q1 2025, the company had $17.4 million in cash, $30.1 million in debt, and net debt of $12.6 million, resulting in a debt-to-equity ratio of 123%. The book value per share stands at $0.91.

Key Observations

Eton’s revenue growth is outpacing nearly all small-cap biotech peers, with the company nearing operational break-even and posting positive EBITDA in several recent quarters. Its leverage, while moderate for a commercial-stage biotech, is considered manageable given its cash balance and pipeline catalysts. The stock trades at a premium for its growth, with a Price/Sales multiple of 9.4×, an EV/Revenue multiple of 9.9×, and a Price/Book multiple of 19×—all below some high-profile rare disease comparables.

Company Overview and Background

Founded in 2017 and headquartered in Deer Park, Illinois, Eton Pharmaceuticals operates a lean commercial model (31 employees as of 2024) focused on developing and commercializing treatments for rare diseases—an attractive, high-margin niche in pharma. Its portfolio includes approved and marketed drugs for severe primary IGF-1 deficiency, adrenal insufficiency, Wilson disease, phenylketonuria, N-acetylglutamate synthase deficiency, homocystinuria, and tyrosinemia type 1, with multiple pipeline programs in late-stage development.

The company’s business model emphasizes in-licensing or acquiring overlooked rare-disease assets, bringing them to market in the US, and investing in specialty field force and digital patient outreach. This capital-efficient approach enables rapid revenue scaling without the capital burn typical of earlier-stage biotech.

Strategic Position and Future Outlook

Growth Drivers

Eton’s growth is fueled by its expanding product portfolio, highlighted by recent launches such as Carglumic Acid and PKU Golike, with additional pipeline assets targeting orphan diseases where competition is limited and payer reimbursement is robust. The company’s multiple late-stage assets—including ET-400, ET-600, Amglidia, ET-700/800, and the ZENEO hydrocortisone autoinjector—provide significant optionality and serve as multi-year catalysts for both organic revenue growth and margin expansion. These advantages are further supported by strong commercial execution, evidenced by double-digit quarterly revenue growth, which is driven by increased product adoption, label expansion, and new patient starts, all managed by a small but highly effective commercial team.

Market Position

Eton operates within a fragmented rare-disease market, enjoying limited direct competition across most of its products. This specialized focus, combined with first-mover advantages in certain therapeutic indications, enables the company to maintain premium pricing and deliver relatively strong gross margins. The company’s pipeline strategy is aimed at both expanding into additional indications and addressing new diseases, thereby increasing its total addressable market and reinforcing a stable, recurring revenue base.

Management & Strategy

The company’s management team brings substantial experience in rare disease commercialization and business development. While recent results demonstrate operational discipline and an ability to execute, risks persist due to the organization’s relatively small scale. Key strategic priorities include expanding commercial reach, pursuing strategic mergers and acquisitions through asset in-licensing, and accelerating the path to profitability.

Pipeline and Recent Developments

Eton’s valuation hinges on its rare-disease pipeline and execution. In the past year, Eton secured FDA approval for ET-400 (hydrocortisone oral solution, now Khindivi), the first-ever oral hydrocortisone solution for pediatric adrenal insufficiency. It also reported a successful pivotal trial for ET-600 (oral desmopressin) to treat pediatric central diabetes insipidus, and submitted an NDA in April 2025. If approved, ET-600 would become the only liquid desmopressin formulation, addressing an unmet need in this population. Eton acquired U.S. rights to Amglidia (glyburide oral suspension), an EMA-approved therapy for neonatal diabetes, and is working with the FDA on its clinical development path. In partnership with Crossject, Eton is advancing ZENEO® Hydrocortisone, a needle-free autoinjector for adrenal crises, with U.S. filings expected by 2026.

Meanwhile, Eton expanded its rare disease portfolio through acquisitions, adding Increlex® (mecasermin) and Galzin® (zinc acetate) in late 2024 to boost its revenue base. Overall, the pipeline progress and strategic deals over the last year suggest a positive outlook for Eton’s future growth.

Competitive and Pipeline Risk Analysis: ET-400 & ET-600

Eton Pharmaceuticals’ ET-400 and ET-600 are poised to address significant unmet needs in pediatric endocrinology. ET-400, a hydrocortisone oral solution for pediatric adrenal insufficiency, has been approved by the FDA as Khindivi™, with patent protection extending to 2043. It complements Eton’s existing product, Alkindi Sprinkle®, which holds orphan exclusivity until 2027. Khindivi™ is indicated for patients aged 5 and older, while Alkindi Sprinkle® is approved for children under 5, allowing Eton to serve a broader pediatric population. Currently, other products on the market include tablets and nasal sprays, neither of which are as easily administered to young patients. More precise dosing is also made possible, which is more important for this patient group.

ET-600, a desmopressin oral solution targeting central diabetes insipidus, has demonstrated pharmacokinetic equivalence to the FDA-approved reference product in a pivotal bioequivalence study involving 75 subjects. Eton submitted a New Drug Application (NDA) for ET-600 in April 2025, with potential approval anticipated in early 2026. If approved, ET-600 would be the only FDA-approved oral liquid formulation of desmopressin, offering precise dosing for pediatric patients. Patent protection for ET-600 extends to 2044.

Both products benefit from strong intellectual property positions and address areas with limited competition. However, execution risks remain, including regulatory approval timelines and post-market safety monitoring. The successful commercialization of ET-400 and potential approval of ET-600 could significantly enhance Eton’s position in the rare disease pharmaceutical sector.

Drivers of ETON’s Stock Upswing and Future Catalysts

ETON Pharmaceuticals’ dramatic share price surge—over 400% in the past year—was driven primarily by a series of clear operational catalysts. Most notably, the company secured multiple FDA approvals and expanded its rare-disease portfolio with assets like ET-400 (Khindivi) for pediatric adrenal insufficiency and Increlex® for severe IGF-1 deficiency, quickly translating regulatory success into new revenue streams. Strong commercial execution, evidenced by more than doubling year-over-year revenues, reinforced investor confidence.

Crucially, ETON’s growth appears sustainable due to a robust pipeline with several late-stage programs: ET-600 (oral desmopressin) awaits FDA review as the only liquid option for pediatric diabetes insipidus, while Amglidia and ZENEO Hydrocortisone represent additional near-term catalysts. Further, ETON’s asset-light, acquisition-driven model enables rapid scaling with minimal capital risk, while market exclusivity and limited direct competition in rare diseases provide durable pricing power.

Looking ahead, upcoming product launches, label expansions, and potential strategic M&A could drive continued upside. Execution risks remain, but ETON’s first-mover advantage in underserved markets and its ability to translate pipeline progress into tangible commercial milestones suggest that its recent stock appreciation is grounded in fundamental, catalyst-driven momentum.

Pipeline Catalysts, Market Opportunity, and Competitive Landscape

ET-600 (Oral Desmopressin): In April 2025 Eton submitted an NDA for ET-600 (an oral desmopressin solution) to treat pediatric central diabetes insipidus. The FDA is applying a standard 10‑month review, targeting a decision and potential Q1 2026 launch. ET-600 addresses roughly 3,000 U.S. pediatric patients with central DI. If approved, it will be the only FDA-approved liquid desmopressin, filling an unmet need (current options are generic tablets or nasal sprays, often requiring compounding for precise pediatric dosing). This formulation enables more accurate, small incremental dosing for children than existing treatments.

Amglidia (Glyburide Suspension): Eton plans an FDA meeting in early 2025 and aims to submit an NDA for Amglidia in 2026. Amglidia is a patented oral glyburide suspension for neonatal diabetes, targeting ~300 U.S. babies; currently no FDA-approved oral treatment exists for this condition. (The standard care is insulin injections or off-label tablets.) If approved, Amglidia could allow many infants—especially those with K_ATP channel mutations—to switch from insulin to a precise liquid therapy. The drug has Orphan Drug status (7-year U.S. exclusivity) and five years of European real-world data, which de-risks approval and supports a premium pricing strategy.

ET-400 / Khindivi™ (Hydrocortisone Oral Solution): Khindivi (formerly ET-400) received FDA approval in May 2025 for children aged ≥5 years with adrenal insufficiency. Notably, this news did not cause a meaningful jump in ETON’s share price – the stock was essentially flat (+0.2%) on the approval announcement ( May 28, 2025 ). This first-in-class 1 mg/mL hydrocortisone solution serves about 5,000 U.S. pediatric patients (age 5–17) with Addison’s disease or congenital adrenal hyperplasia. Unlike competing treatments (e.g. Alkindi Sprinkle for <5 years or adult tablets requiring crushing), Khindivi’s ready-to-use liquid requires no splitting or mixing, enabling precise weight-based dosing. Its entry completes Eton’s pediatric adrenal franchise (supplementing Alkindi) and extends patent/exclusivity protections into the older-child population.ZENEO® Hydrocortisone and ET-800 (Injectable/Auto-Injector): In partnership with Crossject, Eton is developing two adrenal crisis products: a ready-to-use hydrocortisone injectable (ET-800) and a needle-free auto-injector (ZENEO® Hydrocortisone). FDA filings for both are expected in 2H 2026. Together they target a large U.S. adrenal insufficiency market (Eton cites >$200 million total). This breaks down to roughly $100M hospital injectable sales and $100M emergency crisis market. The current hospital standard (Pfizer’s Solu-Cortef®) requires cumbersome multi-step reconstitution; ET-800 will be a premixed liquid alternative.
